text: The surgeon identifies the ileocecal valve and places marking sutures at 100 centimeters and 250 centimeters from the valve. The bowel at the 250-centimeter mark is approximated to the anterior abdominal wall, preparing for the subsequent robotic procedure.

The surgeon dissects the duodenum, exposing at least four centimeters past the pylorus. The gastro-duodenal vessel is identified, and a tunnel is created for the stapler to divide the duodenum. After division, the greater curvature of the stomach is mobilized until the angle of His is identified. A sleeve gastrectomy is performed using linear cutters with a 34 French bougie in position.

The surgeon prepares the first part of the duodenum for anastomosis to the bowel at the 250-centimeter mark. A posterior layer is created using 3-0 absorbable suture, followed by opening the bowel with either a harmonic scalpel or hook cautery. The full thickness layer is initiated posteriorly and continued anteriorly to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the anterior seromuscular layer and divides the bowel at the 250-centimeter mark, separating the biliary limb from the duodenal ileal anastomosis. The alimentary limb is measured and run until the 100-centimeter mark is reached. Enterotomies are created at the 100-centimeter mark and in the biliary limb, followed by a stapled anastomosis that approximates the biliary limb to the common channel at the 100-centimeter mark.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy for the stapler using absorbable suture, then approximates the mesentery of the common channel to the biliary channel with nonabsorbable suture. The procedure concludes with leak checks using methylene blue or endoscopy, followed by removal of specimens and closure of skin incisions.
